body_text:
Residue Theory

EXAMPLE 2 Improper integral of a rational function Evaluate
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)} d x
$$

Solution The integrand satisfies the two conditions of Proposition 2: degr $4 \geq 2+$ degree $p(x)=2$, and the denominator $q(x)=\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)$ has roots. So we may apply (8). We have
$$
\left(z^{2}+1\right)\left(z^{2}+4\right)=(z+i)(z-i)(z+2 i)(z-2 i),
$$
and hence $\frac{1}{\left(z^{2}+1\right)\left(z^{2}+4\right)}$ has simple poles at $i$ and $2 i$ in the upper half-pla ure 5). The residues there are
$$
\operatorname{Res}(i)=\lim _{z \rightarrow i}(z-i) \frac{1}{(z-i)(z+i)\left(z^{2}+4\right)}=\frac{1}{(2 i)(-1+4)}=-\frac{i}{6}
$$
and
$$
\operatorname{Res}(2 i)=\lim _{z \rightarrow 2 i}(z-2 i) \frac{1}{\left(z^{2}+1\right)(z-2 i)(z+2 i)}=\frac{i}{12}
$$

According to (8),
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)} d x=2 \pi i\left(-\frac{i}{6}+\frac{i}{12}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6}
$$

The Substitution $x=e^{t}$
There are interesting integrals of rational functions that are $n$ putable using semi-circular contours as in Example 1. Consider
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d x}{x^{3}+1}
$$

If you try to directly apply the method of Example 1 to this integral, quickly run into problems. First, we are integrating over just half of line, and it is not obvious how to relate this integral to the integral $\int$ Moreover, the latter integral has a problem at $x=-1$. An integral (9) can be solved by using the substitution $x=e^{t}$. We outline th technique in Example 3, where we will compute a more general involving arbitrary powers of $x$. Using the exponential function advantage of eliminating the need to work with powers of $z$, which dealing with branches of the logarithm and can complicate the pro

Changing variables will affect the interval of integration; her change a semi-infinite interval to the real line. If the original in convergent, then it will still be convergent after we change variabl need only check once.

EXAMPLE 3 The substitution $x=e^{t}$
Establish the identity
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{x^{\alpha}+1} d x=\frac{\pi}{\alpha \sin \frac{\pi}{\alpha}}, \quad \alpha \text { any real number }>1
$$
